content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

November 28, 1977

Mr. Clayton P. Roche
Deputy Attorney General
Department of Justice
350 McAllister St., Rm. 6000
San Francisco, California 94102

Dear Mr. Roche:

This letter is in response to your request for our views
on Attorney General Opinion Request No. CV 77/92 II. As will
be discussed later, the Commission has not yet resolved several
of the questions posed in this opinion request. Accordingly,
we request that your opinion be contained in an indexed letter
rather than a published opinion.

The facts, as we understand them, are as follows. Councilman F. Richard "Dick" Griffith of San Bruno, California is
employed by the United States Postal Service, and receives
income from the Service in excess of $10,000. Councilman
Griffith also serves as President of the Postal Workers Union
San Bruno, California local. Councilman Griffith, in his
capacity as President, represents union members in matters
of employment with the Postal Service. On August 22, 1977,
the City Council of San Bruno passed a unanimous resolution
calling for both U.S. Senators from California to take all
possible action to get the Postal Service to locate a classified postal station in downtown San Bruno. Councilman
Griffith participated in the drawing up of this resolution
and voted for its passage. Two issues are presented by
these facts. The first is whether Councilman Griffith's
activities were prohibited by the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act because of his
union affiliations. Second, were his activities prohibited
by the conflict of interest provisions of the Act because
of his employment with the U.S. Postal Service?

Clayton P. Roche
November 28, 1977

Page Two

Government Code Section 87100 1/ provides:

No public official at any level of state
or local government shall make, participate
in making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a govern-
mental decision in which he knows or has
reason to know he has a financial interest.

Government Code Section 87103 provides:

An official has a financial interest in
a decision within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

....
(c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the
regular course of business, aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
received by or promised to the public offi-
cial within twelve months prior to the time
when the decision is made;...

We have been informed that Councilman Griffith does not
receive $250 or more in income from his union. The disqual-
ification provision set forth in Section 87100 will not apply
to Councilman Griffith unless this threshold requirement has
been satisfied. Therefore, Councilman Griffith need not dis-
qualify himself from voting on the resolution because of his
union affiliations.

Remaining is the issue whether Councilman Griffith should
have disqualified himself because of his employment with the
Postal Service.

The salary that Councilman Griffith receives from the
federal government falls within the definition of income as
defined in Section 87630. Councilman Griffith's salary from
the Postal Service is in excess of $10,000. Therefore, Sec-
tions 87100 and 87103(c) may be applicable to the Councilman.
Before these sections will be applied, it must be determined
that Councilman Griffith participated in a governmental deci-
sion within the meaning of Section 87100, and that it was
reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material
financial effect on his source of income.

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Clayton P. Roche
November 28, 1977                                    Page Three


     We are of the opinion that voting on the resolution
constitutes making a governmental decision within the meaning of Section 87100. The Commission has provided by
Regulation 18700(b)(1) that:

     A public official "makes a governmental
decision," .... when he or she, acting within
the authority of his or her office:

     (1) Votes on a matter; ....


     The resolution passed on a vote of the City Council and,
therefore, Section 18700(b)(1) applies. Our conclusion is
bolstered by Section 91003(b) which lists "resolutions" among
the official actions which can be reversed if a court finds a
violation of Section 87100. Section 91003(b) provides:

     If it is ultimately determined that a
violation has occurred and that the offi-
cial action might not otherwise have been
taken or approved, the court may set the
official action aside as void. The offi-
cial actions covered by this subsection
include, but are not limited to orders,
permits, resolutions and contracts, ....

                                        [Emphasis added.]


     Having concluded that Councilman Griffith participated
in a governmental decision, the next question is whether it
was reasonably foreseeable at the time the decision was made
that his action would have a material financial effect, dis-
tinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on his
source of income. In an opinion requested by Tom Thorner 2/,
the Commission addressed the question of foreseeability in a
similar context.

     In example (b), McPhail's is preparing
or has made a bid to supply one or more
of its products, but no award has yet
been made. It is possible, of course,
that there could be special circumstances
present which would indicate that there


_____________
2/ Opinion requested by Tom Thorner, on behalf of the
Board of Directors, Marin Municipal Water District, 1 FPPC
Ops. 198 (No. 76-089, December 4, 1975).

Clayton P. Roche
November 29, 1977                                                Page Four


     is only a remote likelihood of McPhail's
being awarded a supply contract. For
example, McPhail's might have a reason
for making a bid even though it is clear
the contract will be awarded elsewhere.
Under such circumstances, no financial
effect on McPhail's would be reasonably
foreseeable and Director MacPhail would
not be disqualified from participation
in the variance decision.

     As a general rule, however, when the
bid is made with a serious hope that the
contract will be awarded to McPhail's,
we think a financial effect on McPhail's
is reasonably foreseeable even if there
is substantial competition. The statute
requires foreseeability, not certainty.
Furthermore, the fact that a seriously
competitive bid on the project is being
prepared or has been made is likely to
focus the attention of the Director on
the fact that he may benefit if a var-
iance is granted.


     Accordingly, in the instant case, we think foreseeability
exists if the resolution is proposed with a serious hope that
it will lead to the creation of a classified postal station
even if the likelihood of the Postal Service actually acting
on the City Council's resolution is uncertain.

     The final question is whether a material financial effect
on his source of income is present to warrant disqualification.
The Commission has offered guidelines for the purposes of deter-
mining when a material financial effect may be present. Regula-
tion 18702(b)(3) provides that consideration should be given to:

     (b) In determining the existence of a
material effect upon a financial interest,
consideration should be given, but not be
limited to, an analysis of the following
factors:

               ....

     (3) In the case of a source of income
of a public official as defined in Govern-
ment Code Section 87101(c):

Clayton P. Roche
November 28, 1977                                    Page Five


                    (A) The decision will affect the source
                of income in the manner described in sub-
                section (b)(1) above;

                    (B) Whether the governmental decision
                will directly affect the amount of income
                to be received by the official;

                    (C) Whether there is a nexus between
                the governmental decision and the purpose
                for which the official receives income. 3/


        The first guideline offered by the Commission to determine
materiality is not very helpful when applied to an organization
as vast as the federal government. Moreover, even if section
(b)(1) of the regulation were applied to the federal government
in this case, it is doubtful whether the thresholds set forth
therein would be satisfied. We note this conclusion because
it is our understanding that the Postal Service allocates a
specified portion of its budget to new construction for the
coming year. We think it unlikely that the allocation will
be changed because of the passage of the resolution in question
by the San Bruno City Council.

        The second guideline is more on point. A material finan-
cial effect would be present if it is possible that a new sta-
tion could lead to a promotion, advancement, increases in
salary, or any other type of benefit that would constitute
income as defined in Section 82030. If any of these things
could happen to Councilman Griffith, he must disqualify himself.

        If a nexus exists between the governmental decision Coun-
cilman Griffith participated in and the purpose for which he
receives income, another factor for determining materiality
would be present. There may be a nexus present, depending
on Councilman Griffith's status and duties with the Postal
Service. However, we have not been provided sufficient facts
for the purpose of making this determination.

        In addition to the specific guidelines discussed above,
the Commission has provided a general rule to determine when
a material financial effect is present:

_______________
        37  §2702(b)(3)(A) makes a reference to subsection (b)(1).
Subsection (b)(1) concerns whether the effect of the decision
on the official's source of income meets certain monetary
thresholds.

Clayton P. Roche
November 28, 1977                                   Page Six


     The financial effect of a governmental decision
on a financial interest of a public official is
material if, at the time the official makes, par-
ticipates in making or attempts to use his or her
official position to influence the making of the
decision, in light of all the circumstances and
facts known at the time of the decision, the offi-
cial knows or has reason to know that the existence
of the financial interest might interfere with the
official's performance of his or her duties in an
impartial manner free from bias.

                         2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(a)


     However, in the absence of special circumstances such as
those described in Regulation 18702(b)(3)(B) and (C), we think
that the decisions of a federal employee generally will not
affect the federal government in a material fashion.

     If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate
to contact either me or Dwight Dickerson of the Conflicts of
Interest Division.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director
